summary: Significant high-risk regulatory changes for food seasoning/snack compliance are critical. Japan has implemented new prohibitions on unapproved additives, foreign substances, toxic/harmful substances (including in packaging), and pathogens, along with strict limits for agricultural chemical residues, signaling a positive list approach. Singapore now requires all food additives to be SFA-assessed and permitted, prohibits non-compliant additives/contaminants, and mandates detailed ingredient/additive labeling, with specific rules for preservatives in kuehs. Taiwan expanded mandatory allergen declarations to 10 specific items and sulfites (≥10 mg/kg). The United States introduced new action levels for total aflatoxins (20 ppb) and strengthened requirements for allergen preventive controls, 'gluten-free' claims, and mandatory labeling for major food allergens, cochineal extract/carmine, FD&C Yellow No. 5, and sulfiting agents (≥10 ppm). Thailand also prohibits the production, import, or sale of specific foods, requiring official list consultation.

changes.parameter: Unapproved additives (excluding natural flavoring agents and generally consumed items used as additives)
changes.newLimit: Prohibited, unless MHLW specifies as unlikely to cause harm
changes.notes: This implies a positive list system for approved additives.

changes.newLimit: Prohibited if likely to cause harm due to contamination or addition of foreign substances

changes.parameter: Residues of agricultural chemicals, feed additives, and pharmaceutical products for animals
changes.notes: This refers to the 'Positive List System for Agricultural Chemical Residues in Food'.

changes.newLimit: Prohibited, unless specified by MHLW as unlikely to cause harm

changes.parameter: Toxic or harmful substances in apparatus, containers, and packaging
changes.newLimit: Prohibited if likely to cause harm to human health or have a harmful effect on food/additives
Prohibited if contaminated or suspected to be contaminated and likely to cause harm

changes.parameter: Class II chemical preservatives (e.g., sorbic acid, benzoic acid)
changes.newLimit: Not to be directly added during manufacturing of kueh; permitted in fillings and toppings within prescribed limits
changes.notes: This rule is specific to the manufacturing and use of preservatives in kuehs.
All food businesses offering food for sale in Singapore must comply with regulatory limits on food additives.

changes.newLimit: Must be assessed and permitted by SFA
changes.notes: Food additives must be assessed for safety by SFA before they can be used in food sold in Singapore.
Food products containing an additive that is not permitted must not be imported or sold.
Only food additives that have been assessed by SFA are permitted for use in food products sold in Singapore.

changes.newLimit: Must be within SFA’s regulatory limits
changes.notes: Contaminants in food sold in Singapore must be within SFA’s regulatory limits.
Food or food products containing contaminants or incidental constituents that exceed regulatory limits must not be imported or sold.

changes.newLimit: Must be declared in list of ingredients on food labels
changes.notes: Applies to prepacked foods for sale in Singapore.
Prepacked food products must comply with general labelling requirements.

changes.parameter: Crustacean shellfish, mango, peanuts, sesame seeds, milk and goat milk, eggs, tree nuts, gluten-containing cereals, soybeans, fish
changes.newLimit: Mandatory declaration if present as ingredient or food additive
changes.notes: These 10 allergens require mandatory labeling on prepackaged foods if present as an ingredient or food additive.
Mandatory declaration if the residue sulphur dioxide concentration is 10 mg/kg or more.

changes.newLimit: Prohibited from production, import, or sale
changes.notes: A list of foods prohibited from production, import, or sale must be consulted under 'อาหารที่ห้ามผลิต นำเข้า หรือจำหน่าย'.

changes.newLimit: Prohibited from use in food, as listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189). Verification required.
changes.notes: Substances listed as "PROHIBITED" or "PROHIBITED WITH EXCEPTIONS".
The nine major food allergens are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame.
This is a Guidance Level.
This is an Action Level.
This is an Action Level.

changes.newLimit: Verification required against FDA final rules for "gluten-free" definition and compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods.
changes.notes: FDA issued a final rule defining "gluten-free" for food labeling and compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods.

The CGMP & PC rule (21 CFR part 117) establishes requirements for allergen preventive controls to prevent allergen cross-contact in manufacturing and packaging and to prevent undeclared allergens. Facilities must have written procedures.
Must be declared on the label as they are identified as allergenic substances.

The food source of a major food allergen must appear in parentheses following the ingredient name OR immediately after or next to the list of ingredients in a "contains" statement. Specific types of tree nuts, fish species, and Crustacean shellfish species must be declared.
Required to be identified on labels due to potential hypersensitivity reactions.
Must be declared on food labels when present at or above the specified concentration.
The full standard GB 2760-2015 must be consulted for specific additive types, maximum levels, and application scope.

The Codex General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA, Codex STAN 192-1995) sets forth the conditions under which permitted food additives may be used in all foods. Specific provisions are available in the searchable online database, and users are encouraged to consult the Preamble for interpretation.

changes.oldLimit: Verification required via GSFA Online Database
changes.notes: Rule no longer appeared in the latest extraction. Verify whether source changed or extraction missed it.

The Codex maximum level (ML) for a contaminant in a food commodity is the maximum concentration legally permitted. The Codex Committee on Contaminants in Food (CCCF) establishes and endorses these levels, which are detailed in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193-1995).
A Code of Practice (CXC 78-2017) exists for the prevention and reduction of mycotoxins in spices. This document should be consulted for specific guidance.

Specific additive limits for seasoning and snack products must be verified through official documents issued by the Ministry of Health, accessible via the government's legal document portal.

Food businesses must be aware of commonly found contaminants in their products and conduct laboratory tests as needed.

changes.parameter: Cephalopods and cephalopod products; Spiral shells and Spiral shell products thereof; Seeds and seed products (excluding highly refined sunflower seed oil); Kiwis and kiwi products thereof
changes.newLimit: Recommended declaration if present as ingredient or food additive
changes.notes: These allergens are recommended for labeling if present as an ingredient or food additive.

changes.newLimit: Suggested statement if risk of cross-contamination exists
changes.notes: A statement such as 'The plant, equipment or production line used to produce the product is also used to process○○' is suggested.

Such statements are not required by law and should only be used to address unavoidable "cross-contact" after good manufacturing practices have been incorporated. They must be truthful and not misleading.
